notes.anchor: I have set myself certain theses
notes.note: A thesis (Greek thesis) was a set debating-theme of rhetorical training, an abstract general question argued for practice. Cicero turns the schoolroom exercise into therapy and political reflection, framing his own dilemma as a series of impersonal propositions.

notes.anchor: Whether one must remain in one's country when it is under tyranny
notes.note: Each thesis is a thinly veiled question about Cicero's own choice under Caesar, who is never named but is plainly the "tyrant" throughout. Casting them in Greek and in the abstract gave Cicero deniable cover for thoughts dangerous to write plainly in March 49.

notes.anchor: enroll oneself with the best men
notes.note: The "best men" (Greek aristoi, Latin optimates) are the Pompeian senatorial cause. The thesis asks whether one should join them even while disapproving of waging civil war on one's own country --- exactly Cicero's quandary.

notes.anchor: arguing each side in turn, now in Greek, now in Latin
notes.note: Arguing both sides of a question (in utramque partem) was the core of Academic and rhetorical method, which Cicero had absorbed at Athens and Rhodes. Doing it bilingually was both intellectual discipline and a way to numb his anguish.

notes.anchor: he falls upon your very day
notes.note: Cicero hopes the courier will reach Atticus on his birthday, the Nones of March (7 March), making the letter a kind of greeting. The remark dates this letter and ties it to the birthday wishes of the preceding notes.
